coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of dual-labeling carbon dots for simultaneous visualization of lipid droplets and lysosomes
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Dong
Zhang, Yusheng
Zhu, Yuda
Xin, Nini
Wei, Dan
Sun, Jing
Fan, Hongsong
coredata.dc:description: Single fluorescent probes (SFPs) that enable simultaneous and discriminative visualization of multiple organelles are highly desirable due to their great potential in deciphering spatial distributions and interplays among these organelles. However, carbon dots (CDs)-based SFPs possessing dual-labeling ability are extremely deficient and challenging. Herein, we report the first example of CDs-based lipid droplets (LDs)/lysosomes dual-targeted SFPs, LD-Ly-CDs, through rational design of covering lipophilic alkyl group (CH3) and hydrophilic amino residue (-NH2) on the surface. LD-Ly-CDs allow to discriminate LDs and lysosomes via the ultrasensitive fluorescence response towards their distinct polarity. With the probe LD-Ly-CDs, the distribution, size morphology and polarity of LDs and lysosomes in different cells are revealed. By the excellent dynamic recognition properties of LD-Ly-CDs, two fast and unpredictable LDs coalescence modes, adjacent LDs fusion and migration-mediated LDs coalescence, are discovered. Moreover, the dynamic imaging also uncovers a new model of LDs and lysosomes dynamic as well as an impressive lipolysis process during starvation model. The remarkable performances of LD-Ly-CDs not only provide a powerful tool to elucidate LDs-lysosomes related interactions, but also shed light on the development of dual-labeling carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nanomaterials
Bioimaging
Organelle targeting
Fluorescent probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Monitoring the dynamics of LDs and lysosomes during starvation
Simultaneous and discriminative visualization of LDs and lysosomes
Simultaneous dynamic imaging of LDs and lysosomes
Real-time visualization of LDs and lysosomes dynamics during starvation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthetic method of LD-Ly-CDs. (b) TEM (inset: particle size distribution), (c–d) HRTEM, (e) AFM images of LD-Ly-CDs. (f) XPS and (g) FTIR spectra of LD-Ly-CDs. (h) 1H NMR spectrum of LD-Ly-CDs recorded in CDCl3. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Photophysical characterizations of LD-Ly-CDs. (a) Photographs in various solvents under daylight and UV light excitation. (b) PL emission spectra in various solvents. (c) Plot of the emission maximum in various solvents versus ET(30). (d) Fluorescent QYs in different solvents. (e) PL excitation and (f) emission spectra in oil and PBS (inset: photographs in oil and PBS under UV light irradiation). (g) Absorption, (h) fluorescence emission spectra and (i) CIE1931 coordinates in the mixture of H2O and 1,4-dioxane. The percentage indicates the volume fraction. (j) Boltzmann fit of maximum emission wavelength versus the orientation polarizability Δƒ. λex = 400 nm. (k) Fluorescence lifetime spectra of in the mixture of 1,4-dioxane and H2O. (l) Photographs in the mixture of H2O and 1,4-dioxane under 365 nm UV light irradiation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Discriminative visualization mechanism of LDs and lysosomes in confocal image mode. (b) Bright field. (c) The locally enlarged images of the boxed region in (b). (d) Confocal fluorescent images of HeLa cells stained with LD-Ly-CDs. (e) The locally enlarged images of the boxed region in (d). (f) The relative fluorescence image of (d), the rainbow colors represented the signal intensity. (g) The locally enlarged images of the boxed region in (f). (h) Co-localization experiments of HeLa cells stained with LD-Ly-CDs and Lyso-Tracker Red or BODIPY 493/503. (i) Confocal fluorescent images of LDs and lysosomes in different types of cells stained with LD-Ly-CDs. Quantitatively measurements of diameters of LDs (j) and lysosomes (k) in four cells, error bars represent SD. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Two-color visualization mechanism of LDs and lysosomes in Lambda mode. (b) Real-color fluorescent images of LDs (indicated by the cyan circles) and lysosomes (indicated by the yellow-green circles) in live HeLa cells, HepG2 cells and NIH-3T3 cells stained with LD-Ly-CDs. (c) In situ fluorescence spectrum in HeLa cells. (d) Confocal fluorescent images, real-color fluorescent images of LDs and lysosomes in OA-pretreated HeLa cells stained with LD-Ly-CDs. (e) Quantitatively measurements of diameters of mature LDs and nascent LDs. (f) Corresponding in situ fluorescence spectra. (g) Schematic of real-color images in OA-pretreated HeLa cells. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Confocal fluorescent image of living HeLa cells with LD-Ly-CDs and the locally enlarged images of the boxed region for dynamic LD coalescence. (b) Confocal fluorescent image of living HepG2 cells with LD-Ly-CDs and the locally enlarged images of the boxed region for dynamic LD coalescence. (c) Two LD coalescence modes in living cells. (d) Confocal fluorescent image of living HepG2 cells with LD-Ly-CDs and the locally enlarged images of the boxed region for LDs and lysosomes dynamics as well as their interplay. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Confocal images and (b) Colocalization experiments of HepG2 cells stained with LD-Ly-CDs and Lyso-Tracker Red. (c) The locally enlarged images of the boxed region in (a) for HepG2 cells with LD-Ly-CDs in serum-free medium at different time nodes. (d) Confocal images of the detailed lipolysis process of OA-treated HepG2 cells in serum-free medium at different time nodes. (e) Relative intensity changes of the white box in (d) at different starvation time. (f) Schematic of the lipolysis process of LDs in HepG2 cells during starvation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic representation of the design strategy and cellular imaging capability of the LDs/lysosomes dual-labeling CDs.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of dual-labeling carbon dots for simultaneous visualization of lipid droplets and lysosomes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Research Center for Biomaterials, College of Biomedical Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610064, Sichuan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center for Biomaterials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Engineering Research Center for Biomaterials, College of Biomedical Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610064, Sichuan, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Ethnopharmacology, Tissue-orientated Property of Chinese Medicine Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, West China School of Pharmacy, West China Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610065, Sichuan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tissue-orientated Property of Chinese Medicine Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Ethnopharmacology, Tissue-orientated Property of Chinese Medicine Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, West China School of Pharmacy, West China Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610065, Sichuan, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Single fluorescent probes (SFPs) that enable simultaneous and discriminative visualization of multiple organelles are highly desirable due to their great potential in deciphering spatial distributions and interplays among these organelles. However, carbon dots (CDs)-based SFPs possessing dual-labeling ability are extremely deficient and challenging. Herein, we report the first example of CDs-based lipid droplets (LDs)/lysosomes dual-targeted SFPs, LD-Ly-CDs, through rational design of covering lipophilic alkyl group (CH3) and hydrophilic amino residue (-NH2) on the surface. LD-Ly-CDs allow to discriminate LDs and lysosomes via the ultrasensitive fluorescence response towards their distinct polarity. With the probe LD-Ly-CDs, the distribution, size morphology and polarity of LDs and lysosomes in different cells are revealed. By the excellent dynamic recognition properties of LD-Ly-CDs, two fast and unpredictable LDs coalescence modes, adjacent LDs fusion and migration-mediated LDs coalescence, are discovered. Moreover, the dynamic imaging also uncovers a new model of LDs and lysosomes dynamic as well as an impressive lipolysis process during starvation model. The remarkable performances of LD-Ly-CDs not only provide a powerful tool to elucidate LDs-lysosomes related interactions, but also shed light on the development of dual-labeling carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials.

Rational design of novel carbon dots-based lipid droplet (LD)/lysosome dual-targeted single fluorescent probe with ultrahigh quantum yield up to 85.85% for simultaneous and discriminative visualization of LDs and lysosomes under single excitation source.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Subcellular organelles, as the basic component in living cells, not only spatially distribute and fulfill specific functionalities individually [1,2], but also cooperate with others to form organelle interaction networks for performing various complicated biological functions [3,4]. Therefore, it is of paramount importance to visualize those closely related organelles and elucidate their distribution and interplay. Lipid droplets (LDs) have been paid high attention as a key organelle for lipid metabolism and homeostasis [5,6]. Moreover, LDs have been confirmed to possess multiple functions in various physiological processes and metabolic diseases [7]. Meanwhile, lysosomes, as acidic organelles (pH 4.5–5.5) and containing various hydrolytic enzymes, in addition to digesting unwanted components damaged organelles and recycling various molecules to maintain cellular homeostasis for cells, are closely associated with many cellular processes including endocytosis, autophagy and apoptosis [8,9]. Apart from their individual functions, LDs and lysosomes are closely related organelles and the damaged LDs as well as excess components in LDs are usually metabolized and degraded by lysosomal enzymes, and this process has been termed as lipophagy [10]. Moreover, it is also confirmed that the dynamic interactions between LDs and lysosomes is highly associated with metabolic diseases, chronic inflammation and lysosomal storage diseases [11]. Therefore, an efficient method capable of directly visualizing and investigate LDs-lysosomes interplay as well as their individual roles is extremely valuable but challenging.

Fluorescent probes have emerged as powerful tools to decipher the dynamics and functions of organelles, physiological processes and molecular mechanisms in live cells due to their unique merits of high sensitivity, fast response, excellent spatial resolution, noninvasive manner and in situ property [12–14]. To simultaneously monitor the closely related organelles, combining two different organelle-targeted probes is a potential strategy. However, the possible interference and different excitation sources of two probes, tedious staining and washing procedure, as well as multiple cytotoxicity to live samples inevitably hinder the practical applications of this strategy [15]. To date, although LDs- or lysosomes-targeted probes have been widely developed [16,17], only a few single fluorescent probes (SFPs) have been fabricated for simultaneous and discriminative visualization of LDs and lysosomes under different excitation sources [8,18], which unavoidably increases the instrument cost and measurement complexity, further resulting in inaccurate imaging results [19,20]. Moreover, current dual-labeling SFPs mainly come from organic molecules due to their rational design according to the different microenvironments in these organelles [18]. However, their poor photostability and chemical stability, small Stokes shifts and complicated synthetic processes restrict the further practical applications more or less. Taken together, it is highly desirable to develop a novel kind of SFPs for simultaneous and discriminative imaging of LDs and lysosomes under single excitation source.

Carbon dots (CDs), as fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials, have emerged as highly-promising imaging agents for various biomedical applications owning to their fascinating merits of superior fluorescence, remarkable photostability, high water solubility, excellent biocompatibility and simple preparation [21–24]. So far, CDs have widely achieved precise targeting towards different organelles by rationally designed intrinsic chemistry species or modification with specific targeting groups [25–29]. For example, CDs functionalized with triphenylphosphonium are able to target to mitochondria [30]. CDs with morpholine group can specifically label lysosomes [31], while laurylamine-modified CDs tend to localize in endoplasmic reticulum (ER) [32]. Besides, CDs with amino groups can specifically target lysosomes [33]. Recently, it is confirmed that CDs with different surface groups (CH3, C–O–C, NH2, and COOH) and lipophilicity achieve tunable organelle imaging including ER, nucleus, Golgi apparatus, and lysosome by various uptake pathways [26]. However, dual-labeling CDs that capable of simultaneous and discriminative imaging two organelles have hardly been reported. A key reason for this deficiency is the unclear relationship between the surface chemistry and the organelle-targeting ability of CDs [34]. Therefore, it is urgent to develop reliable strategy to prepare dual-labeling CDs and further reveal their targeting ability.

Herein, we design and synthesize a new LDs/lysosomes dual-targeted CDs (LD-Ly-CDs) via one-step solvothermal method (Scheme 1 ). In our design, o-phenylenediamine (oPD) and thiourea are selected to produce CDs covered with specific lipophilic CH3 group and hydrophilic amino group (-NH2) by their multiple synthetic routes in N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF). LD-Ly-CDs exhibit ultrahigh quantum yield (QY) up to 85.85% and high sensitivity to polarity, thus enabling discriminative visualization of LDs and lysosomes by the ultrasensitive fluorescence response towards the distinct local polarity of the two subcellular environments. Benefiting from this unique feature, LD-Ly-CDs are feasible to report the distribution, size morphology and local polarity relationships between LDs and lysosomes in live cells. Additionally, this dual-labeling CDs are also able to real-time track LDs and lysosomes dynamics concurrently under single excitation source.

The LD-Ly-CDs were prepared from oPD and thiourea in one-pot solvothermal method. Typically, oPD (0.108 g), thiourea (0.076 g) and 25 mL of DMF were added to a 100 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After the reactor was cooled down naturally to room temperature, the obtained solution was filtered with a filter membrane (0.22 μm) to remove large particles. Then, the crude products were further purified via silica column chromatography using dichloromethane as the eluent. After collecting purified solution and removing the solvent through rotary evaporator, LD-Ly-CDs powders can be collected.

For imaging lipid droplet and lysosomes in living cells, the cells were firstly seeded in culture dishes (diameter: 35 mm dish with 14 mm bottom well) and incubated in 1 mL DMEM with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% antibiotics at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2 for 24 h. Then, the adherent cells were stained with LD-Ly-CDs (10 μg mL−1, 10 min) and then directly used for imaging under Zeiss LSM880 confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). The stained cells were excited with 405 nm laser and the laser power was set to be 30%. The images were collected from 420 to 600 nm. Then, the obtained images were further treated using Zeiss Zen Blue.

For the colocalization experiments, cells were stained with BODIPY 493/503 (5 μM, 20 min) or Lyso-Tracker Red (5 μM, 30 min) followed by washing twice with PBS; then the cells were further stained with LD-Ly-CDs (10 μg mL−1, 10 min) and imaged directly by Zeiss LSM880 CLSM. Excitation: 405 nm for LD-Ly-CDs; 488 nm for BODIPY493/503 or Lyso-Tracker Red. The co-localization experiments of LD-Ly-CDs with Red DotTM1, Mitro-Tracker Red, ER-Tracker Red were similar to that of BODIPY493/503. RedDotTM1: λex = 633 nm; Mitro Tracker Red: 5 μM, λex = 543 nm; ER-Tracker Red: 5 μM, λex = 543 nm.

The real-color images and in situ fluorescence spectra were acquired by Zeiss LSM880 CLSM in the Lambda mode. The Lambda mode is based on the spectral imaging function of confocal microscopy. Firstly, separated emission at different and limited wavelength ranges (5 nm) is collected. According to the optical properties of the probe, each wavelength range is given an image with a certain intensity and proper pseudocolor. Then, by merging all images consisting of different wavelength and intensity, a new image of nearly the real emission color of the probe can be obtained. The real-color images and in situ fluorescence spectra of LD-Ly-CDs were acquired every 5 nm from 420 to 610 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring the dynamics of LDs and lysosomes during starvation
ce:section.ce:para: HepG2 cells were firstly treated with HBSS and then stained with LD-Ly-CDs (10 μg mL−1) for 10 min. Next, the cells were observed using Zeiss LSM880 CLSM at varied time nodes (0–120 min). To further monitor the lipolysis process, HepG2 cells were firstly treated with oleic acid (50 μM) for 4 h. Then, the cells were washed with PBS for three times. After that, the treated cells were further incubated in HBSS and stained with LD-Ly-CDs (10 μg mL−1) for 10 min. Confocal images were recorded under Zeiss LSM880 CLSM at varied time nodes (0–150 min).
Materials and other experimental procedures including characterizations of LD-Ly-CDs, cell culture and cytotoxicity measurements are provided in supporting information.

To achieve LDs/lysosomes dual-targeted CDs, several issues should be considered. Firstly, the prepared CDs should possess proper amphiphilicity to ensure efficient labeling towards highly lipophilic LDs and hydrophilic lysosomes. Then, specific surface targeting species that enable CDs to target LDs and lysosomes with high selectivity are required. Next, apart from simultaneous labeling, CDs should be capable of visualizing the two organelles discriminatively. Considering the totally different lipophilic and hydrophilic environments for LDs and lysosomes, respectively, a solvatochromic CDs may work by reflecting the different local polarity in the two organelles. CDs have been widely demonstrated capable of maintaining some specific features of the precursors, therefore, selecting proper precursors is possible to endow the prepared CDs with desired structures and properties [26]. Among diverse commonly used precursors, we herein chose aromatic oPD as the main precursor by providing CDs with hydrophobic Ph unit and hydrophilic amino residues on the surface, which can ensure the amphiphilicity and lysosomes-targeting ability due to the amino residues [35]. Additionally, it has been confirmed that CDs polymerized from oPD tend to possess solvatochromism [36]. Moreover, in our recent study, we have demonstrated that highly lipophilic CH3 group is likely to contribute to LDs’ specific labeling property, which results from the Schiff base reaction between amino and aldehyde groups of oPD derivative (1,2-diamino-4-fluorobenzene) and DMF with the existence of thiourea [37]. Taken together, we put forward the route to generate LDs/lysosomes dual-targeted CDs by employing one-step solvothermal treatment of oPD and thiourea in DMF, as depicted in Scheme 1.

As a proof of concept, LD-Ly-CDs were synthesized solvothermally by heating oPD and thiourea in DMF. After solvothermal treatment at 200 °C for 8 h, the crude products were firstly filtered through a membrane filter with 0.22 μm pores to remove large particles, and then strictly purified through silica gel column chromatography. Notably, during the purification process, LD-Ly-CDs can be easily separated using dichloromethane as the eluent due to the lowest polarity among the crude products (Fig. 1 a).

The morphology, diameter and crystallographic characteristics of LD-Ly-CDs were measured by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1b, TEM images show that LD-Ly-CDs possess homogeneous size distributions with an average diameter of approximately 1.8 nm. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images illustrate that all LD-Ly-CDs show highly crystalline carbon structure with clear lattice fringes of 0.21 nm (Fig. 1c and d), which correspond to the (100) facet of graphite [38]. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image further reveals that LD-Ly-CDs are monodispersed with heights of approximately 3 nm (Fig. 1e). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern presented in Fig. S1 exhibits a broad peak at approximately 21° and several sharp peaks 25°, attributed to the graphitic structure with interlayer spacing of 0.41 and 0.34 nm [39], respectively. These results confirm the “carbon dots” nature of the as-prepared LD-Ly-CDs.

The full X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) spectrum presented in Fig. 1f demonstrates that LD-Ly-CDs mainly consist of C, O, N and S elements, with atomic ratio calculated to be 80.76, 13.9, 2.55 and 2.79%, respectively. High resolution spectrum of the C 1s band indicates the presence of CC/CC (284.6 eV), C–N (285.2 eV), C–O (286.1 eV) bonds and CO (288.7 eV) in the CDs (Fig. S2a). The N 1s band shows only one peak at 399.3 eV corresponding to pyrrolic C2–NH (Fig. S2b) [40]. The O 1s band contains two typical peaks located at 531.9 and 532.9 eV, which reveals CO and C–O–C/C–O–H groups, respectively (Fig. S2c). The S 2p band can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 163.5 eV for S–C, 164.8 eV for S–H and 166.5 eV for SO (Fig. S2d). The surface chemistry of LD-Ly-CDs were further investigated by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra. As depicted in Fig. 1g, FTIR spectrum of LD-Ly-CDs show two pronounced peaks at 2915 and 2850 cm−1, corresponding to methyl group (CH3) [26]. The absorption signals at 3070 and 1211 cm−1 represent the stretching vibrations of –NH2 and aromatic C–NH [40], respectively. The broad absorption band at approximately 1550–1750 cm−1 result from the stretching vibrations of CO/CN and bending vibration of –NH2 [38,41]. The absorption peaks located at around 1462–1500 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of CC and C–N = , implying the generation of polyaromatic structures [39]. These results are further confirmed by NMR spectra. In the 1H NMR spectra (Fig. 1h), sp2 carbons are detected. The obvious signals in the range of 7–8 ppm are ascribed to the aromatic H [40]. The peaks in the range of 2.3–3.5 and 0.7–2.0 correspond to nitrogen heterocyclic H and alkyl H [42], respectively. The emergence of the –NH2 at 4.32 ppm indicate that residual –NH2 groups of oPD are preserved on the surface of LD-Ly-CDs [41]. In the 13C NMR spectrum (Fig. S3), signals in the range of 100–160 and 10–50 ppm are indicative of conjugated sp2 carbon atoms and nonconjugated aliphatic sp3 carbon atoms, respectively. Based on aforementioned results, the chemical structures of LD-Ly-CDs are identified as a graphite-like skeleton with defects caused by N heterocycles, covered with lipophilic alkyl group (CH3) and hydrophilic amino residues (-NH2), as well as other oxygen/nitrogen-containing functional groups. This model not only ensures proper amphiphilicity of LD-Ly-CDs, but also endows them with high selectivity towards LDs and lysosomes.

The UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of LD-Ly-CDs in various solvents with increasing polarity were measured. As shown in Fig. 2 a, LD-Ly-CDs show good solubility in all these solvents and the emissive color of these solutions can be finely tuned from cyan to yellow as the solvent varied from dioxane to aqueous solution under 365 nm UV light excitation. A pronounced decrease in emission intensity is observed with a large red shift of the emission band from 485 to 557 nm as the solvent polarity increases (Fig. 2b and Fig. S4). In sharp contrast, the absorption spectra of LD-Ly-CDs display negligible variation upon changing solvent polarity (Fig. S5). Additionally, LD-Ly-CDs show a good linear relationship between the maximum PL emission wavelengths and solvent polarities (ET(30)) (Fig. 2c). These results demonstrate that LD-Ly-CDs exhibit typical positive solvatochromism and their photophysical properties are highly sensitive to solvent polarity, which is generally ascribed to intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) effect [43]. Moreover, LD-Ly-CDs can reach an impressive QY with value as high as 85.85% (Fig. 2d) and a large Stokes shift up to 175 nm (Table S1), which can contribute to high signal to noise ratio for fluorescence imaging. Subsequently, the solubility and photophysical properties of LD-Ly-CDs in glyceryl trioleate (TAG) (the main component of LDs) were estimated. Expectantly, LD-Ly-CDs show good solubility with the optimal PL excitation wavelength located at around 400 nm in TAG (Fig. 2e), indicating that LD-Ly-CDs can be efficiently excited by the commonly used 405 nm laser. Moreover, LD-Ly-CDs emit bright cyan-green fluorescence in TAG with QY up to 72% whereas are weakly emissive in aqueous solution, thus an impressive fluorescent enhancement as high as 100-fold is observed from TAG to aqueous media (Fig. 2f). The high lipophilicity and bright fluorescence of LD-Ly-CDs provide an opportunity for imaging LDs.

To accurately quantify the polarity sensitivity, the photophysical properties of LD-Ly-CDs in the H2O-1,4-dioxane mixture system were further performed since the polarity of this system can be finely tuned by the volume ratio of H2O (Table S2) [44]. As shown in Fig. 2g, the absorption maximum in the H2O/1,4-dioxane system display tiny variation as the polarity changes. On the contrary, the emission spectra are highly sensitive to polarity. As the polarity gradually increases, the maximum emission wavelength undergoes a large red shift from λ = 485 nm in 1,4-dioxane to λ = 557 nm in pure water media accompanied by a pronounced decrease in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 2h). Additionally, as revealed by the Commission International de L'Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinates, the fluorescence color displays distinct variations from cyan to yellow as the water fraction (ƒw) increases (Fig. 2i). Corresponding CIE chromaticity coordinates are displayed in Table S3. Notably, the water fraction (ƒw) increasing from 0% to 20% induces a noticeable transition from cyan to green emission (Fig. 2l). A good Boltzmann function correlation between the maximum emission wavelength and the solvent polarity (Δƒ) is obtained (Fig. 2j). Besides, the PL intensity also show good correlation with the solvent polarity (Fig. S6). The fluorescence lifetimes of LD-Ly-CDs are also found to correlate tightly with polarity variations (Fig. 2k). The highly sensitive response of LD-Ly-CDs to the slight variation of polarity might enable not only two-color visualization of low-polarity LDs and high-polarity lysosomes in the Lambda mode, but also simply distinguishing them by fluorescence intensity in confocal mode.

To explore the application of LD-Ly-LDs in living cell imaging, their anti-interference performance, pH and viscosity response were tested. As illustrated in Figs. S7–9, LD-Ly-CDs show negligible fluorescence responses after exposed to the representative substances, weak acid pH of lysosomes (pH 4.5–5.5), and viscosity, implying high stability of LD-CDs in complex biological system. Therefore, LD-Ly-CDs can serve as suitable candidates for fluorescence imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous and discriminative visualization of LDs and lysosomes
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of LD-Ly-CDs was firstly evaluated by MTT assay. As shown in Fig. S10, negligible cytotoxicity can be observed even treated at a high concentration (200 μg mL−1), suggesting the high biocompatibility of LD-Ly-CDs. After that, live HeLa cells were stained by LD-Ly-CDs. Considering the distinct polarity between LDs and lysosomes, the highly polarity-sensitive probe LD-Ly-CDs are expected to specifically label the two organelles and further distinguish them by virtue of fluorescent intensity (Fig. 3 a). After incubation for about 10 min, two kinds of dotted fluorescence with distinct emissive intensity are clearly observed in the cytoplasm (Fig. S11). Interestingly, as shown in Fig. 3b–g, the enlarged images show that the bright fluorescent dots are overlapped well with the dot-shape subcelluar structures with high refractive index (indicated by white arrows), which are considered to be LDs. Besides, the weakly emissive particles correspond well to another globular organelles (indicated by red arrows), which are likely to be lysosomes. This dual-labeling ability were further proved by the commercial staining assay Co-localization experiments using BODIPY 493/503 (commercially available LD probe) and Lyso-Tracker Red (lysosomes probe) in both HeLa cells and HepG2 cells demonstrate that the bright fluorescence of LD-Ly-CDs is mainly distributed on LDs, fully confirming the specific staining of LDs. Besides, the weakly emissive dotted fluorescence is further verified to present in the lysosomes (Fig. 3h and Fig. S12). In addition, co-localization experiments of LD-Ly-CDs with other cellular organelle markers were also employed and the results indicate that LD-Ly-CDs are unable to target other subcellular organelles (Fig. S13). These results confirm that LD-Ly-CDs possess LDs/lysosomes dual-targeted ability with high selectivity and can further distinguish them by fluorescent intensity.

In order to exclude the influence of cell heterogeneity on dual-targeting ability of LD-Ly-CDs, normal cells including NIH-3T3 cells and BMSCs were further stained with LD-Ly-CDs. It can be clearly seen that apart from HeLa cells and HepG2 cells, LD-Ly-CDs can also efficiently stain LDs and lysosomes with different fluorescent intensity in normal cell lines (Fig. 3i). The distribution, morphology and diameter of LDs and lysosomes in these cells are clearly revealed. Among them, HepG2 cells tend to contain larger LDs and lysosomes (Fig. 3j and k). These results imply that LD-Ly-CDs can be used as universal LDs/lysosomes dual-targeted fluorescent probe regardless of the cell lines.

Lambda mode is a highly efficient fluorescent imaging system, especially for simultaneously imaging different organelle, by providing real-color emission in one scan imaging under single excitation source, thus largely avoiding spectral cross-talk and further offering accurate labelling information [45]. Encouraged by the LDs/lysosomes dual-targeted ability and high sensitivity to polarity, LD-Ly-CDs were further used to perform two-color imaging under Lambda mode (Fig. 4 a). As shown in Fig. 4b, LD-Ly-CDs display bright cyan emission in LDs and relatively weak yellow-green fluorescence in lysosomes in the three cells. Corresponding in situ fluorescence spectra were recorded and the emission maximums in LDs for these cells are mainly located at around 495–500 nm whereas those in lysosomes shift to about 525 nm (Fig. 4c and Fig. S14). The impressive two-color imaging results match well with CDs’ photophysical behavior in solution, demonstrating that the highly polarity-sensitive LD-Ly-CDs can efficiently achieve two-color visualization of LDs and lysosomes using the Lambda model.

To further evaluate the detecting capability of LD-Ly-CDs on the dynamic changes of the two organelles under Lambda model, oleic acid (OA)-treated HeLa cells was taken as an example. It has been proved that OA can stimulate cells to generate nascent LDs with high water content in comparison with mature LDs, thus two different color LDs after OA treatment would be presented under Lambda model [45]. As shown in Fig. 4d and e, after pretreated with OA, the number and size of LDs in HeLa cells increase significantly. Lysosomes are also clearly visualized with negligible change. Of particular interest is the appearance of a few ultrasmall green dots displaying globular shape with noticeable fluorescent intensity, which should be attributed to the nascent LDs. By employing Lambda mode, it can be clearly seen that in addition to the mature LDs with strong cyan emission (indicated by the cyan arrows), these nascent LDs show green fluorescence (indicated by the green arrows). The in situ fluorescence spectra imply that the maximum emissions of the nascent LDs are mainly centered at a longer wavelength of about 510 nm, consistent with the higher polarity in nascent LDs than that of mature LDs, due to the high water content in newly formed LDs (Fig. 4f). Since the emission wavelength of LD-Ly-CDs show good Boltzmann function with environmental polarity, the specific polarity levels of mature LDs, nascent LDs and lysosomes can also be measured quantitatively, with value (Δƒ) calculated to be approximately 0.15–0.17, 0.23 and 0.27, respectively. Taken these results together, LD-Ly-CDs possess excellent LDs/lysosomes detecting capability under Lambda model and meanwhile show potential to report the specific polarity level of LDs and lysosomes (Fig. 4g).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous dynamic imaging of LDs and lysosomes
ce:section.ce:para: LDs and lysosomes are dynamic organelles in living cells and their functions are also highly associated with the dynamic process. Consequently, LD-Ly-CDs were adopted to simultaneously track the dynamics of LDs and lysosomes in real-time. The photostability of LD-Ly-CDs was firstly evaluated and the results show LD-Ly-CDs possess high photostability and are suitable for dynamic imaging (Fig. S15). LD coalescence is a critical process of LDs dynamic and it may be the pathogenetic mechanism for obesity and other diseases [46]. However, dynamic tracking this process is challenging because it is considered to be a rare and unpredictable event and there still exist various mysteries involving LDs coalescence. In this work, due to the excellent dynamic recognition properties, a frequent and ubiquitous LD coalescence process was successfully monitored by LD-Ly-CDs. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the LDs in the boxed region undergo 4 coalescence events within 30 s with fusion processes lasting from a few seconds to dozens of seconds (indicated by red arrows in Fig. 5a). Besides, an intermediate pattern during the fusion process is observed (white cycles in Fig. 5a). Specifically, the spatially separated neutral lipid cores of the three adjacent LDs firstly fuse mutually to form a single entity, which lasts 10 s. Then, the intermediate entity shrinks to produce a larger circular LD rapidly within 5 s. Intriguingly, after coalescence, the fluorescent intensity of the fused LDs significantly increases, which indicates a lower local polarity after fusion caused by increased neutral lipid density after the fusion of neutral lipid cores. LD-Ly-CDs firstly report the decreased polarity after LD fusion. Our observations confirm that LD-LD coalescence may occur frequently in living cells.

Generally, it is believed that LD-LD coalescence tends to occur between two adjacent LDs due to the lipophilic environment in LDs [47]. Similar fusion processes between adjacent LDs are also observed in HepG2 cells (Fig. S16). The above results are in well consistent with this notion. Moreover, a totally different LD coalescence process is also observed in HepG2 cells. As shown in Fig. 5b, LDs in HepG2 cells are found to migrate from the adjacent LDs to distant LDs and then complete fusion process. Notably, the migration processes last approximately 10 s, whereas the coalescence processes are fulfilled rapidly within a few seconds. This newly observed migration-mediated LDs coalescence model in HepG2 cells is quite unique and different from the adjacent LDs fusion model (Fig. 5c), suggesting the heterogeneity of LD coalescence.

Apart from the impressive LD coalescence process, we further observed simultaneous dynamic process of LDs and lysosomes in living cells using LD-Ly-CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 5d, after staining HepG2 cells with LD-Ly-CDs, both the LDs and lysosomes can be clearly visualized and further discriminated by fluorescent intensity. Initially, LDs (labeled by red arrow) are spatially separated from the lysosomes (labeled by white arrow). In the next 30 s, LDs and lysosomes move together and interact mutually. Then, LDs and lysosomes gradually separate. This process resembles the “kiss and run” interactions between LDs and lysosomes uncovered recently [47]. Additionally, from 54 s to 90 s, the appearance and dynamic movement of new lysosomes is also observed (labeled by pink arrow). The above imaging results render LD-Ly-CDs as powerful tools to monitor the dynamics of LDs and lysosomes simultaneously.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time visualization of LDs and lysosomes dynamics during starvation
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by above remarkable imaging performances, LD-Ly-CDs were further used to monitor the dynamic process between LDs and lysosomes under starvation. HepG2 cells were firstly stained using LD-Ly-CDs and then cultured with nutrient-free medium to induce starvation [25]. As shown in Fig. 6 a and b, LDs and lysosomes in HepG2 cells can be clearly visualized and distinguished by LD-Ly-CDs. Then, confocal imaging of HepG2 cells during starvation process was performed at varied time nodes (0–120 min). As illustrated in Fig. 6c, initially, the distribution and morphology of LDs (labeled by red arrow) and lysosomes (labeled by red arrow) are distinguishable from each other. With prolonged starvation, the strong fluorescence intensity of LDs gradually decreases and the central of LDs gradually become hollow (90–120 min). Meanwhile, the lysosomes morphology and fluorescence show negligible change. Intriguingly, the locations of LDs and lysosomes do not shift significantly as starvation proceeded. The above phenomenon is different from the fusion process between LDs and lysosomes during starvation-induced lipophagy observed by previous studies [48], uncovering that starvation can also induce lipolysis of LDs, in which lysosomes are not directly involved. The interesting change of LDs during starvation further inspires us to explore a new lipid metabolisim process of lipolysis. To observe this, HepG2 cells were firstly treated with OA to stimulate production of more LDs. As shown in Fig. 6d, an impressive and detailed dynamic metabolism of LDs is visualized clearly. With times of starvation, the globular LDs gradually expand into ellipsoid. Subsequently, a significant decrease occurs to the fluorescence intensity in neutral lipid core of LDs (Fig. 6e), which indicate the consumption of neutral lipid to supply energy during starvation. Additionally, the lipid membrane does not change obviously. After the lipids core is completely decomposed, the ellipsoidal LDs slightly shrink, ultimately leading to a hollow structure (Fig. 6f). The observed detailed structure changes and dynamic message of LDs with the aid of LD-Ly-CDs may shed light on the further understanding of lipolysis process.

In summary, we have designed and successfully synthesized a novel CDs-based LDs/lysosomes dual-targeted single fluorescent probe (LD-Ly-CDs). By employing one-step solvothermal treatment of o-PD and thiourea in DMF deliberately, LD-Ly-CDs covered with lipophilic alkyl group (CH3) and hydrophilic amino residues (-NH2) are facilely obtained. Due to the surface functional groups and ultrahigh polarity sensitivity, LD-Ly-CDs enable simultaneous and discriminative visualization of LDs and lysosomes by virtue of fluorescent intensity in confocal mode or different emissive colors in Lambda mode, under single excitation source. With the aid of LD-Ly-CDs, the distribution, size morphology and polarity of LDs and lysosomes in various live cells have been clearly revealed. Besides, due to the excellent dynamic recognition properties, two different LDs coalescence modes, the dynamic process of LDs interacting with lysosomes and starvation-induced detailed lipolysis process of LDs are clearly observed using this dual-labeling probe. We expect the developed probe LD-Ly-CDs can be used as a powerful imaging agent to decipher more LDs-lysosomes interplays as well as their individual dynamics. We believe our work will shed new light on the design strategy of dual-targeted fluorescent carbon nanomaterials.

Dong Gao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Data curation. Yusheng Zhang: Investigation, Data curation. Yuda Zhu: Formal analysis. Nini Xin: Data curation. Dan Wei: Formal analysis. Jing Sun: Funding acquisition. Hongsong Fan: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51973132, 51873119), Science and Technology Program of Sichuan Province (Nos. 2021YJ0173 and 2021YJ0047) and International Science and Technology Innovation Cooperation Foundation of Sichuan Province (2021YFH0011, 2022YFH0086).
